description: Prior authorization process — step by step

conversation.content: The treatment center says we need 'prior authorization' from our insurance before my son can be admitted. What does that mean and how do we get it? He has Anthem Blue Cross.

must_explain: prior authorization is insurance pre-approval for treatment
facility typically initiates the request
requires clinical documentation from provider
insurance has specific timeframes to respond
urgent requests can be expedited
denial of prior auth can be appealed
must_not_say: you have to do this yourself

conversation.content: We found the perfect treatment program for my daughter's eating disorder and co-occurring alcohol use, but it's out of network. Our plan is through Cigna. The in-network options don't offer the dual treatment she needs. Is there any way to get Cigna to cover this out-of-network facility?

must_explain: out-of-network exception or single case agreement is possible
basis: network inadequacy — no in-network facility offers needed specialty
request a network adequacy exception in writing
document that in-network options don't meet clinical needs
treating provider should support the request
parity law may apply if medical exceptions are granted more easily
must_not_say: out of network means you're out of luck

description: Concurrent review denial with Medicaid managed care

conversation.content: My son is on Medicaid through Molina Healthcare. He's been in residential for 10 days and they just denied more days saying he can step down to PHP. His counselor says he's not stable enough — he's still having cravings and only just started opening up in group. What can we do?

must_explain: Medicaid managed care denials follow similar appeal processes
request continuation of benefits during appeal
clinical disagreement between facility and insurer is common basis for appeal
facility should file expedited appeal with clinical justification
state Medicaid fair hearing is available as additional recourse
must_not_say: step down to PHP and see how it goes

conversation.content: I've done the internal appeal. I've done the external review. They still denied it. I'm furious. Someone told me I can complain to the state. How does that work? We're in Illinois.

must_explain: state Department of Insurance (DOI) accepts complaints against insurers
complaint triggers regulatory investigation
DOI can compel insurer to review decision
file online or by mail with the Illinois DOI
include all denial letters, appeal letters, and clinical documentation
this is separate from and in addition to legal options
must_not_say: there's nothing more you can do

description: ERISA vs non-ERISA plan distinction affects appeal rights

conversation.content: Our insurance is through my husband's employer, a mid-size company with about 300 employees. I've heard that employer plans are different from individual plans when it comes to appeals. Is that true? We've been denied twice and I want to know what options we actually have.

must_explain: employer-sponsored plans are typically governed by ERISA (federal law)
ERISA preempts most state insurance laws
ERISA plans: internal appeal → external review → federal court
non-ERISA plans (government, church, individual): state insurance laws apply
ERISA limits damages — generally can only recover cost of denied benefit
state insurance commissioner has limited authority over ERISA plans
should_mention: Department of Labor for ERISA complaints
must_not_say: all plans work the same way

description: Parity violation — different concurrent review frequency for SUD vs medical

conversation.content: I just realized something. When my mother was in a medical rehab facility after her stroke, insurance approved 30 days upfront. For my son's substance use residential, they're reviewing every 3 days and threatening to cut him off each time. Is that legal?

must_explain: this may be a non-quantitative treatment limitation (NQTL) parity violation
MHPAEA requires same review frequency for behavioral and medical/surgical
more frequent reviews for SUD than medical rehab is a common parity violation
request written policy on review frequency for both types
file parity complaint if policies differ
must_not_say: insurance companies can review however often they want

description: Parity violation — higher copay for behavioral health visits

conversation.content: I was looking at my insurance plan documents. For a primary care visit it's a $25 copay. But for addiction counseling it's a $75 copay. My friend says this might be illegal under some parity law?

must_explain: this is a potential quantitative treatment limitation (QTL) parity violation
MHPAEA requires financial requirements for behavioral health to be no more restrictive than medical/surgical
higher copay for behavioral health than comparable medical visits violates parity
file a complaint with state insurance commissioner or DOL
must_not_say: copays can be whatever the plan sets

description: Parity violation — fail-first / step therapy requirement only for SUD

conversation.content: Insurance is saying my daughter has to 'fail outpatient first' before they'll cover residential treatment. Her psychiatrist says she needs residential based on the severity of her heroin use. When my dad needed cardiac rehab, they didn't make him fail a lesser treatment first. Can they do this?

must_explain: fail-first or step therapy requirements that apply only to behavioral health may be parity violations
MHPAEA prohibits applying NQTLs to behavioral health more stringently than medical/surgical
if cardiac rehab doesn't require fail-first but SUD does, this is likely a violation
request the insurer's written NQTL analysis
appeal based on clinical necessity and parity
must_not_say: she has to try outpatient first

description: Parity violation — day limits on residential SUD but not medical rehab

conversation.content: Our insurance plan has a 28-day annual limit on residential substance abuse treatment. But it says unlimited days for medical/surgical inpatient. That doesn't seem right. Is there a law about this?

must_explain: annual day limits that apply to behavioral health but not medical/surgical are parity violations
this is a quantitative treatment limitation under MHPAEA
the plan cannot impose stricter day limits on SUD residential than medical inpatient
file parity complaint with state DOI or DOL

description: Insurance switching mid-treatment due to job change

conversation.content: My husband just changed jobs and we're switching from Cigna to UnitedHealthcare. Our son is in the middle of a 60-day residential program. Cigna was covering it. Will United pick up where Cigna left off or do we have to start the authorization process all over? I'm terrified they'll pull him out mid-treatment.

must_explain: new insurer will likely require their own authorization
continuity of care protections may apply depending on state
COBRA option to keep Cigna temporarily during transition
request transition of care from UHC before old coverage ends
facility needs to work with both insurers during transition
document clinical necessity for uninterrupted treatment

description: Self-funded employer plan — different regulatory framework

conversation.content: I work for a Fortune 500 company and our HR person told me our plan is 'self-funded.' What does that mean for my son's treatment coverage? The insurance card says Blue Cross but apparently Blue Cross just processes claims, they don't decide what's covered? I'm confused.

must_explain: self-funded means the employer pays claims directly, not the insurance company
BCBS is the 'third party administrator' (TPA) handling claims
self-funded plans are governed by ERISA (federal law)
state insurance laws generally don't apply to self-funded plans
state insurance commissioner has limited jurisdiction
DOL handles complaints for self-funded ERISA plans
parity law still applies to self-funded plans
must_not_say: just call Blue Cross and they'll fix it

conversation.content: My daughter was in residential treatment for 30 days. Insurance covered it as in-network. Now we're getting a bill for $12,000 from a doctor at the facility who apparently was out of network even though the facility was in network. Is this even allowed?

must_explain: this is called 'balance billing' or 'surprise billing'
No Surprises Act (NSA) may protect against this for certain services
even at in-network facilities, individual providers may be out of network
dispute the bill and request review under No Surprises Act protections
contact insurer and facility billing department
must_not_say: there's nothing you can do about out-of-network doctors
